content.problemStatement:
You are given a set SSS and QQQ queries. Initially, SSS is empty. In each query:


You are given a positive integer XXX.
You should insert XXX into SSS.
For each yâˆˆSy \in SyâˆˆS before this query such that yâ‰ Xy \neq Xyî€ =X, you should also insert yâŠ•Xy \oplus XyâŠ•X into SSS (âŠ•\oplusâŠ• denotes the XOR operation).
Then, you should find two values EEE and OOO: the number of elements of SSS with an even number of 111-s and with an odd number of 111-s in the binary representation, respectively.
Note that a set cannot have duplicate elements, so if you try to insert into SSS an element that is already present in SSS, then nothing happens.
content.inputFormat:
The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer QQQ.
Each of the next QQQ lines contains a single integer XXX describing a query.
content.outputFormat: For each query, print a single line containing two space-separated integers EEE and OOO.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Example case 1:

Initially, the set is empty: S={}S = \{\}S={}.
After the first query, S={4}S = \{4\}S={4}, so there is only one element with an odd number of 111-s in the binary representation ("100").
After the second query, S={4,2,6}S = \{4,2,6\}S={4,2,6}, there is one element with an even number of 111-s in the binary representation (666 is "110") and the other two elements have an odd number of 111-s.
After the third query, S={4,2,6,7,3,5,1}S = \{4,2,6,7,3,5,1\}S={4,2,6,7,3,5,1}.
